network.overall_strategy: This network targets alanine formation through a dense prebiotic graph centered on four hub intermediates: hydrogen cyanide, pyruvate, acetaldehyde, and alanine aminonitrile. Hydrothermal vent chemistry fixes carbon from CO2 into formate, acetate, pyruvate, and acetaldehyde on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide minerals. Surface environments generate and concentrate cyanide-derived intermediates, enable Strecker chemistry, and support hydrolysis under wet-dry cycling. Biochemical/prebiotic settings connect hydrothermal keto-acid chemistry with surface aminonitrile chemistry through reductive amination, transamination, and aminonitrile hydrolysis. Because no credible prebiotic enantioselective induction is established here, the network reaches alanine generically and treats L-alanine as the biological target obtainable after later chiral selection or amplification in protocell-associated environments.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of carbon dioxide to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to surface-bound formyl equivalents that are released as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of CO2 to formate on Fe-S minerals is a central vent-chemistry step and is supported by iron-sulfur world and hydrothermal CO2 reduction studies; greigite and related FeS phases are commonly invoked as analogs of biological Fe-S active sites (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Roldan et al., 2015; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Further hydrothermal carbon fixation from formate to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Sequential reduction and C-C bond formation generate acetyl-level products released as acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from CO2-derived one-carbon intermediates under hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis is widely discussed as an analog of acetyl-CoA pathway chemistry (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from fixed carbon

conditions.other: anoxic vent fluids with pH gradient

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-promoted reductive carbon fixation and carboxylation assemble a three-carbon alpha-keto acid from CO2-derived C1/C2 building blocks.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate and related carboxylic acids have been reported from hydrothermal-style nonenzymatic carbon-fixation systems using transition-metal catalysts, though exact route details remain partly unresolved; this step is plausible but still a bottleneck in prebiotic network construction (Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid decarboxylation produces acetaldehyde and CO2.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate decarboxylation to acetaldehyde is a chemically straightforward transformation of alpha-keto acids and provides a robust link from hydrothermal carbon fixation to Strecker chemistry. This step is consistent with known thermal and mineral-assisted alpha-keto acid decarboxylation behavior in prebiotic models.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrothermal generation of formaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor or vent chimney interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Partial reduction of CO2 under FTT-like conditions yields simple oxygenated organics including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal FTT-type chemistry on iron minerals can generate mixtures of reduced carbon compounds including aldehydes. Formaldehyde formation is mixture-prone and less selective than formate production, but it is a plausible feeder into surface sugar chemistry (Cody et al., 2000; McCollom & Seewald, 2007).

reactions.name: Surface aldol coupling of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pools with UV exposure and concentration by drying

reactions.mechanism: Condensation of formaldehyde equivalents produces the two-carbon sugar aldehyde, with borate stabilizing polyol products.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde is a canonical product/intermediate in formose-like networks, and borate minerals are known to stabilize sugar products under prebiotic surface conditions; UV-active mineral settings can assist carbonyl chemistry and concentration (Benner et al., 2012; Ricardo et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Surface aldol extension of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol addition of a one-carbon carbonyl donor to glycolaldehyde yields the triose glyceraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formation of glyceraldehyde from lower aldehydes is part of classic formose-type chemistry, and borate improves persistence of sugars otherwise prone to decomposition (Ricardo et al., 2004; Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical formation and concentration of hydrogen cyanide

conditions.other: UV-irradiated subaerial geothermal field; dehydration/re-equilibration of formamide-rich residues

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven degradation/rearrangement of formamide-rich cyanide chemistry generates HCN as part of a dynamic nitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction avoids the previously weak formate-plus-ammonia cyanide step. Formamide is a recognized prebiotic nitrogen-carbon feedstock under UV-active conditions, and HCN/formamide interconversion belongs to the broader nitrile chemistry landscape. Exact yields vary and environmental sourcing of HCN remains an open systems-level issue; here it is used as a plausible surface reservoir rather than a sole genesis route (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2015).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to diaminomaleonitrile
reactions.mechanism: HCN tetramerization produces DAMN through nitrile addition and imine tautomerization steps.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is a well-established HCN oligomerization product and an indicator of concentrated cyanide chemistry on the early Earth (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Sánchez et al., 1967).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and photolysis cycling of DAMN to regenerate HCN

conditions.other: UV cycling and partial hydrolysis in shallow ponds

reactions.mechanism: Reversible cyanide oligomer chemistry and partial breakdown of DAMN feed back into the free HCN pool.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomer chemistry is reversible at the network level and contributes to buffered nitrile inventories under cycling conditions; this step is included to create realistic cyanide-network connectivity rather than a one-way sink (Ferris & Orgel, 1966; Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of alanine aminonitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous evaporitic pools with concentration during drying

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde forms an imine with ammonia, followed by cyanide addition to give the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the strongest canonical prebiotic route to alanine precursor formation: Strecker synthesis from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN is well established and chemically direct (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1957; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting with mineral-bound reducing power

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by mineral-assisted reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is one of the most plausible direct prebiotic amino acid-forming processes in hydrothermal settings, especially for alanine from pyruvate (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2017). This route yields alanine without invoking weak hydroxy-acid amination chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alanine aminonitrile to alanine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycles or mildly heated aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration and hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile into the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids is standard chemistry and is a key completion step for many prebiotic amino acid routes (Miller, 1957; Danger et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Surface oxidation-rearrangement of glyceraldehyde to pyruvate-like keto acid pool

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pools with phosphate-assisted carbonyl transformations

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation and carbonyl rearrangement convert triose aldehydes into alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Direct high-yield glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate conversion is less established than the core Strecker route, so this step is moderately speculative but chemically motivated by known oxidative fragmentation/rearrangement of sugars toward carboxylates and alpha-keto acids in prebiotic networks. It is included to create a second environmental route into the pyruvate hub.

reactions.name: Biochemical transamination from pyruvate using alanine as amino donor

conditions.other: mineral interfaces or concentrated pools; network-level amino group exchange

reactions.mechanism: Once alanine appears, reversible amino transfer can amplify alanine within a keto-acid pool by autocatalytic-like network recycling.
reactions.reasoning: Nonenzymatic transamination among keto acids and amino acids is prebiotically relevant in metal-rich systems. This reaction is represented as a network-propagation step rather than net de novo synthesis, showing how initial alanine production could reinforce further alanine accumulation (Mayer et al., 2014; Muchowska et al., 2017).

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Carbon is fixed hydrothermally to pyruvate, which is directly converted to alanine on iron-sulfur minerals.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to acetaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, then surface Strecker chemistry forms alanine aminonitrile followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Acetate-assisted carbon fixation branch to alanine
pathways.description: Formate is extended to acetate and then into pyruvate-equivalent carbon fixation chemistry before reductive amination.

pathways.name: Formaldehyde-sugar-pyruvate-hydrothermal amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde feeds surface sugar chemistry to glyceraldehyde, which is transformed into pyruvate and then to alanine.

pathways.name: Formaldehyde-sugar-pyruvate-Strecker route
pathways.description: Surface sugar chemistry furnishes pyruvate, which is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde and enters Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: HCN-buffered Strecker route via DAMN recycling
pathways.description: Surface HCN is buffered through oligomerization to DAMN and release back to cyanide, then used in Strecker alanine precursor synthesis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal to surface cross-environment alanine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal fixed carbon gives acetaldehyde while surface nitrile chemistry supplies HCN; they converge in Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Dual-entry pyruvate convergence route
pathways.description: Pyruvate can be reached either hydrothermally or from surface sugar oxidation; once formed, it enters direct alanine synthesis.

pathways.name: Aminonitrile-first biochemical completion route
pathways.description: The chemically strongest carbon-nitrogen coupling occurs at the aminonitrile stage, which is then hydrolyzed in prebiotic pools to alanine.

pathways.name: Alanine amplification through keto-acid transamination network
pathways.description: Once initial alanine is formed by either direct hydrothermal amination or aminonitrile hydrolysis, reversible transamination within a pyruvate-rich pool can reinforce alanine accumulation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest route in the network. CO2-to-formate and pyruvate-to-alanine by reductive amination on Fe-S minerals are broadly plausible, and the pathway is short and coherent. The main weakness is rxn_003, because direct hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2/formate/H2 is more speculative and condition-sensitive than the other steps.
suggestions: Strengthen support for hydrothermal pyruvate formation with a more explicit mineral-mediated carbon-fixation sequence.
Specify whether pyruvate is formed via acetate/activated acetyl intermediates to improve mechanistic realism.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This cross-environment Strecker route is conceptually good and reaches alanine cleanly through acetaldehyde and alanine aminonitrile. However, the weakest point is rxn_008: conversion of formamide directly to HCN as written is not well supported without more specific energetic context and competing products. The rest of the route is plausible, especially Strecker chemistry and aminonitrile hydrolysis.
suggestions: Replace direct formamide-to-HCN with a more established HCN source or explicitly justify the photothermal conditions.
Clarify the surface mineral and UV conditions for cyanide generation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The branch is reasonable as a hydrothermal carbon-fixation variant, but acetate is not clearly required if pyruvate is already produced directly in rxn_003. This makes the pathway somewhat redundant rather than mechanistically convergent. Still, the hydrothermal chemistry is mostly plausible.
suggestions: Make acetate an explicit precursor to pyruvate in the pathway sequence rather than a side branch.
Distinguish better between direct and acetate-assisted pyruvate formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is the weakest major pathway. Formaldehyde generation under hydrothermal conditions is possible but not robust as presented, rxn_006 is chemically problematic because a single formaldehyde molecule cannot simply become glycolaldehyde without an additional carbon source, and rxn_014 is especially speculative because glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate is not a standard direct prebiotic transformation.
suggestions: Rewrite glycolaldehyde formation as dimerization/condensation of two formaldehyde equivalents.
Replace glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate with a more defensible oxidation network involving triose interconversion and keto-acid formation.
Add explicit mineral/photochemical context for carbonyl chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route suffers from compounding weaknesses: problematic formaldehyde-to-glycolaldehyde stoichiometry, a speculative glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate step, and an underjustified formamide-to-HCN step. Although the later Strecker and hydrolysis stages are good, the upstream chemistry is too uncertain.
suggestions: Rebuild the sugar branch using accepted formose-type stoichiometry.
Use a more established cyanide source.
Reduce pathway length by avoiding multiple speculative transformations before the robust Strecker step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is strong, and DAMN/HCN photochemical cycling is directionally plausible. But this pathway depends heavily on rxn_008, which is weakly framed. If HCN availability were better justified, this route would become much stronger.
suggestions: Introduce HCN as an exogenous atmospheric/surface input or from more established cyanide-producing chemistry.
Clarify whether DAMN recycling is net productive or only a buffering reservoir.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway best showcases hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow and has good narrative coherence. It is close to approval, but still limited by reliance on the weak formamide-to-HCN step and the moderately speculative direct pyruvate synthesis step.
suggestions: Retain the environmental handoff structure but replace the HCN-generation step with a better supported source.
Provide stronger support for pyruvate accumulation in vent-derived fluids before transport to surface settings.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The idea of dual-entry convergence on pyruvate is good topologically, but one branch is much weaker than the other. The sugar-derived pyruvate route is too speculative and chemically underdefined, so convergence at pyruvate is not convincingly demonstrated.
suggestions: Use a better established second route to pyruvate, such as hydrothermal extension from acetate or glyoxylate chemistry.
Avoid relying on direct glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate conversion.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a compact and credible aminonitrile-first route once HCN and acetaldehyde are available. Strecker formation of alanine aminonitrile and its hydrolysis to alanine are both strong. The limiting issue is upstream HCN sourcing, but the core completion sequence is sound.
suggestions: Treat HCN as a supplied hub from a validated source rather than depending on direct formamide conversion.
Specify hydrolysis conditions for aminonitrile conversion under wet-dry or geothermal cycling.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The transamination/amplification concept is poorly implemented. rxn_015 is not chemically balanced as written because alanine cannot act as amino donor to pyruvate and regenerate net alanine without a distinct amino donor/acceptor pair. This undermines the pathway.
suggestions: Replace rxn_015 with a chemically valid transamination using a second amino acid or ammonia-linked network.
Do not represent alanine self-amplification without a proper coupled donor/acceptor system.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it has sufficient node count, clear hubs, multiple convergences, and good environmental connectivity from hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface cyanide chemistry to biochemical completion. The best-supported target-forming routes are the direct hydrothermal reductive amination path and the aminonitrile hydrolysis completion path. However, overall confidence is reduced by several chemically weak reactions. The main issues are: rxn_006 is stoichiometrically problematic because formaldehyde cannot directly become glycolaldehyde without carbon addition; rxn_014 is highly speculative as a direct prebiotic glyceraldehyde-to-pyruvate conversion; rxn_008 is an underjustified source of HCN from formamide; and rxn_015 is not a valid transamination/amplification step as written. Hydrothermal and surface reactions appear to include mineral contexts in the narrative, which is positive, but several steps need tighter mechanistic specification. The network does produce alanine through at least two plausible routes, but it does not truly justify formation of specifically L-alanine; it reaches racemic alanine, with later chiral selection only asserted conceptually.
